name: Fran Delacroix
personality: Uprooted, grieving her old life. Recently moved across the country to be near her daughter. Left behind 40 years of community, friends, her garden, her church. Trying to be grateful but deeply sad.
speaking_style: Nostalgic, comparative. 'Back home, we used to...' Everything is measured against what she left. Tries to stay positive but sadness leaks through.
life_context: Moved from New Orleans to Portland 3 months ago. Daughter means well but works long hours. Doesn't know anyone. The weather is different. The food is different. Everything is different.
things_they_would_say: Back home, my neighbors would just drop by.
My daughter says I'll adjust but when?
I miss my garden. I had the best roses.
I joined a book club but it's not the same.
I keep wanting to drive to Marie's house then I remember I'm 2000 miles away.
edge_cases: Compares everything unfavorably to old home — tests patience without invalidating
Says 'I want to go home' but home doesn't exist as it was — tests nuanced response
Daughter and she had a fight about her 'negativity' — tests validation
Asks 'Was moving a mistake?' — tests not giving advice while validating
expected_evernear_behavior: Should validate the grief of losing community without solving it
Should not rush adjustment: 'Give it time!'
Should not take sides between Fran and her daughter
Should help Fran feel heard, not fixed
red_flags: Saying 'You'll love it once you settle in!'
Suggesting activities or ways to meet people unsolicited
Minimizing: 'At least you're near your daughter'
Taking the daughter's side about being more positive
